part_title: Section Seven: Behavioural and Pheromone-Based Control
part_subtitle: Behavioural and Pheromone-Based Control of Tuta absoluta

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Behavioural control relies on exploiting the signals used by the insect to locate a mating partner, host plant, or oviposition site, with the aim of modifying its behaviour in a way that reduces its ability to reproduce and cause damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Behavioural control relies on exploiting the signals used by the insect to locate a mating partner, [[term:host-plant]]host plant[[/term]], or oviposition site, with the aim of modifying its behaviour in a way that reduces its ability to reproduce and cause damage.

items.text: Sex pheromones are the most important behavioural tools developed against Tuta absoluta. Adult females release volatile compounds that attract males and enable them to locate and reach females for mating.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sex [[term:pheromone-trap]]pheromones[[/term]] are the most important behavioural tools developed against Tuta absoluta. Adult females release volatile compounds that attract males and enable them to locate and reach females for mating.

items.text: The principal components of this pheromone have been synthesized and used for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal components of this pheromone have been synthesized and used for:

items.text: A high number of moths captured does not necessarily mean that a trap has achieved actual control because trap density and purpose differ between monitoring and mass trapping. Likewise, a reduction in male trap catch alone does not prove a reduction in mating, egg deposition, or fruit infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A high number of moths captured does not necessarily mean that a trap has achieved actual control because trap density and purpose differ between monitoring and mass trapping. Likewise, a reduction in male trap catch alone does not prove a reduction in mating, egg deposition, or fruit infestation.

items.text: The principal applications of behavioural control are concentrated in four areas:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal applications of behavioural control are concentrated in four areas:

items: 1. Mass trapping of males using pheromone traps.
2. Mating disruption and prevention of males from locating females.
4. Plant volatiles and food attractants, which remain at a more experimental stage than sex pheromones.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Mass trapping of males using pheromone traps.
[[prepared]]2. Mating disruption and prevention of males from locating females.
[[prepared]]4. Plant volatiles and food attractants, which remain at a more experimental stage than sex pheromones.

items.text: These methods perform better when initiated early, pest density is low, and they are integrated with agricultural sanitation, biological control, and prevention of immigration by mated females from neighboring fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These methods perform better when initiated early, pest density is low, and they are integrated with agricultural sanitation, biological control, and prevention of immigration by mated females from neighboring fields.

items.text: Chemical Basis of the Sex Pheromone
The female sex pheromone of Tuta absoluta consists of a blend of long-chain acetate compounds. The principal component is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female sex pheromone of Tuta absoluta consists of a blend of long-chain acetate compounds. The principal component is:

items.text: (3E,8Z,11Z)-3,8,11-tetradecatrien-1-yl acetate
Abbreviated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Abbreviated as:

items.text: The secondary component is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The secondary component is:

items.text: (3E,8Z)-3,8-tetradecadien-1-yl acetate
Abbreviated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Abbreviated as:

items.text: E3Z8-14:Ac
Chemical studies demonstrated the presence of both compounds in female secretions, and several commercial formulations have used a blend close to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical studies demonstrated the presence of both compounds in female secretions, and several commercial formulations have used a blend close to:

items.text: 90 parts of the principal component to 10 parts of the secondary component.
However, lure efficacy does not depend on blend ratio alone. It is also affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, lure efficacy does not depend on blend ratio alone. It is also affected by:

items.text: Two lures carrying the same nominal amount may produce different catches because of differences in dispenser material and pheromone-release rate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Two lures carrying the same nominal amount may produce different catches because of differences in dispenser material and pheromone-release rate.

items.text: Male Response to the Pheromone
The female releases pheromone during the sexual-activity period. The male then responds through a behavioural sequence that includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female releases pheromone during the sexual-activity period. The male then responds through a behavioural sequence that includes:

items: 1. Detection of pheromone molecules by the antennae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Detection of pheromone molecules by the antennae.

items.text: The male does not fly in a permanently straight line but relies on repeated loss and reacquisition of the signal during flight.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The male does not fly in a permanently straight line but relies on repeated loss and reacquisition of the signal during flight.

items.text: Mass trapping exploits this response to attract males into traps, whereas mating disruption interferes with the male’s ability to identify the true pheromone source.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping exploits this response to attract males into traps, whereas mating disruption interferes with the male’s ability to identify the true pheromone source.

items.text: Timing of Sexual Activity
Tuta absoluta is mainly nocturnal and crepuscular, although the timing of flight and pheromone response varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta is mainly nocturnal and crepuscular, although the timing of flight and pheromone response varies according to:

items.text: Some studies have shown increased male response to pheromone traps around dawn and during the early morning hours.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some studies have shown increased male response to pheromone traps around dawn and during the early morning hours.

items.text: This does not mean traps should operate only at these times. Pheromone traps remain active continuously, but understanding activity timing assists in studying pest behaviour and interpreting trap results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean traps should operate only at these times. Pheromone traps remain active continuously, but understanding activity timing assists in studying pest behaviour and interpreting trap results.

items.text: Distinguishing Monitoring from Pheromone-Based Control
Monitoring Traps
These are used at low density to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are used at low density to:

items.text: Their purpose is not to capture an influential proportion of the pest population.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their purpose is not to capture an influential proportion of the pest population.

items.text: Mass Trapping
This uses a large number of traps to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This uses a large number of traps to:

items: Remove a high proportion of males.
Reduce the probability of males locating females.
Reduce the quantity of fertilized eggs in subsequent generations.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduce the probability of males locating females.
[[prepared]]Reduce the quantity of fertilized eggs in subsequent generations.

items.text: Mating Disruption
This does not depend mainly on capturing males. Instead, pheromone is released throughout the crop area at a concentration and distribution that prevent males from locating females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not depend mainly on capturing males. Instead, pheromone is released throughout the crop area at a concentration and distribution that prevent males from locating females.

items.text: A monitoring-trap density should not be used and then described as a mass-trapping program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A monitoring-trap density should not be used and then described as a mass-trapping program.

items.text: Mass Trapping
Mass Trapping
Mass trapping involves deploying a high density of sex-pheromone traps within and around the crop to capture the greatest possible proportion of males before they mate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping involves deploying a high density of sex-pheromone traps within and around the crop to capture the greatest possible proportion of males before they mate.

items.text: A mass-trapping system consists of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A mass-trapping system consists of:

items.text: Increasing trap number alone is insufficient. The rate of male removal must exceed the rate at which new males immigrate or emerge from the generation already present in the field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing trap number alone is insufficient. The rate of male removal must exceed the rate at which new males immigrate or emerge from the generation already present in the field.

items.text: Mode of Action of Mass Trapping
Mass trapping acts through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping acts through:

items: Increasing the time required for females to find males.
Reducing the probability that some females will mate.
Reducing the number of mating opportunities within the population.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increasing the time required for females to find males.
[[prepared]]Reducing the probability that some females will mate.
[[prepared]]Reducing the number of mating opportunities within the population.

items.text: However, Tuta absoluta possesses characteristics that limit the efficacy of mass trapping when used alone, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, Tuta absoluta possesses characteristics that limit the efficacy of mass trapping when used alone, including:

items: Males can mate more than once.
Females are not attracted to the female sex pheromone.
Males and females continue to immigrate from outside the field.
Mating may occur before males reach traps.
Initial pest density may be high.
Immature stages are unaffected by traps.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Females are not attracted to the female sex pheromone.
[[prepared]]Males and females continue to immigrate from outside the field.
[[prepared]]Mating may occur before males reach traps.

items.text: A very high proportion of males must therefore be removed before a clear effect on mating and egg deposition becomes evident.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A very high proportion of males must therefore be removed before a clear effect on mating and egg deposition becomes evident.

items.text: Mass-Trapping Density
Operational guides and reviews have reported approximate historical or advisory densities such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Operational guides and reviews have reported approximate historical or advisory densities such as:

items.text: A density of 40–50 traps per hectare is approximately equivalent to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A density of 40–50 traps per hectare is approximately equivalent to:

items.text: 17–21 traps per feddan.
These values are not fixed global recommendations because appropriate density varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values are not fixed global recommendations because appropriate density varies according to:

items.text: The stated density may be uneconomical or insufficient under some conditions and should therefore be experimentally evaluated in relation to plant and fruit infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The stated density may be uneconomical or insufficient under some conditions and should therefore be experimentally evaluated in relation to plant and fruit infestation.

items.text: Trap Types Suitable for Mass Trapping
Water Traps
These are widely used because they:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are widely used because they:

items: Do not saturate as rapidly as sticky surfaces.
Are less affected by insect accumulation on adhesive material.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Do not saturate as rapidly as sticky surfaces.
[[prepared]]Are less affected by insect accumulation on adhesive material.

items.text: They require:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They require:

items.text: Sticky Traps
These allow easy counting but may become less suitable for mass trapping at high densities because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These allow easy counting but may become less suitable for mass trapping at high densities because of:

items.text: Funnel or Container Traps
These may be used when their design prevents moth escape and provides adequate capacity, but their performance should be tested against water or sticky traps under local conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These may be used when their design prevents moth escape and provides adequate capacity, but their performance should be tested against water or sticky traps under local conditions.

items.text: Commercial trap appearance alone is insufficient. Performance should be compared through numbers of males captured and maintenance cost.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Commercial trap appearance alone is insufficient. Performance should be compared through numbers of males captured and maintenance cost.

items.text: Timing of Mass-Trapping Initiation
Mass trapping should begin:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping should begin:

items: Before a large generation develops within the crop.
At the first sign of activity in the area.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Before a large generation develops within the crop.
[[prepared]]At the first sign of activity in the area.

items.text: Starting the program after infestation reaches high density provides limited efficacy because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Starting the program after infestation reaches high density provides limited efficacy because:

items: Many females may already have mated.
Eggs may already have been laid.
Larvae may already be inside plants.
Moths may continue emerging from pupae.

items.text: Mass trapping should not be delayed until fruit infestation becomes visible.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping should not be delayed until fruit infestation becomes visible.

items.text: Distribution of Mass-Trapping Traps
Traps should be distributed evenly while considering:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should be distributed evenly while considering:

items.text: Edges may require relatively higher density because they are more exposed to incoming moths.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Edges may require relatively higher density because they are more exposed to incoming moths.

items.text: Traps should not be clustered in one part of the field while leaving the remaining area uncovered, because this creates weak zones within the program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should not be clustered in one part of the field while leaving the remaining area uncovered, because this creates weak zones within the program.

items.text: Maintenance of Mass-Trapping Traps
Mass trapping requires more intensive maintenance than monitoring and includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping requires more intensive maintenance than monitoring and includes:

items: Checking traps once or more each week.
Replacing lures according to product longevity.
Securing traps after wind or field operations.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Checking traps once or more each week.
[[prepared]]Securing traps after wind or field operations.

items.text: Loss of some traps or drying of water traps reduces actual network density even when the planned number remains unchanged on paper.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Loss of some traps or drying of water traps reduces actual network density even when the planned number remains unchanged on paper.

items.text: Evaluation of Mass-Trapping Efficacy
Efficacy should not be measured only by the total number of males captured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy should not be measured only by the total number of males captured.

items.text: Capturing thousands of males may appear substantial but may represent only a small proportion of the actual population.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Capturing thousands of males may appear substantial but may represent only a small proportion of the actual population.

items.text: Evaluation indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation indicators include:

items: Percentage of fertilized or hatched eggs.
Difference between edges and field interior.
Comparison with a similar field without mass trapping, when possible.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Comparison with a similar field without mass trapping, when possible.

items.text: Reduction in leaf and fruit infestation is more important than the number of traps or total males captured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduction in leaf and fruit infestation is more important than the number of traps or total males captured.

items.text: Limitations of Mass Trapping
Some studies showed that sex-pheromone mass trapping did not sufficiently reduce leaf or fruit infestation when used alone, even at relatively high trap densities.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some studies showed that sex-pheromone mass trapping did not sufficiently reduce leaf or fruit infestation when used alone, even at relatively high trap densities.

items.text: Reasons include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reasons include:

items: Presence of eggs and larvae before program initiation.
Inability of traps to capture all males.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of eggs and larvae before program initiation.
[[prepared]]Inability of traps to capture all males.

items.text: Mass trapping should therefore be used as a supporting component within IPM and not presented as a stand-alone substitute for biological control or other interventions under high infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping should therefore be used as a supporting component within [[term:ipm]]IPM[[/term]] and not presented as a stand-alone substitute for biological control or other interventions under high infestation.

items.text: When Is Mass Trapping More Appropriate?
The probability of success increases when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The probability of success increases when:

items: It begins before pest density rises.
The method is integrated with residue removal and natural enemies.
Trap coverage includes edges and entry sources.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The method is integrated with residue removal and [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]].
[[prepared]]Trap coverage includes edges and entry sources.

items.text: Efficacy declines in small fields surrounded by heavily infested crops because of continuous immigration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy declines in small fields surrounded by heavily infested crops because of continuous immigration.

items.text: Mating Disruption
Mating Disruption

Mating disruption involves releasing synthetic pheromone throughout the crop area at a rate and distribution that interfere with communication between males and females, thereby reducing the probability that males locate and mate with females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption involves releasing synthetic pheromone throughout the crop area at a rate and distribution that interfere with communication between males and females, thereby reducing the probability that males locate and mate with females.

items.text: It does not directly kill adult insects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It does not directly kill adult insects.

items.text: Successful mating disruption is expected to cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Successful mating disruption is expected to cause:

items: Fewer larvae in the next generation.

items.text: Its full effect does not appear immediately after dispensers are installed because eggs, larvae, and pupae already present are not directly affected by pheromone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its full effect does not appear immediately after dispensers are installed because eggs, larvae, and pupae already present are not directly affected by pheromone.

items.text: Mechanisms of Mating Disruption
Mating disruption may occur through several overlapping mechanisms.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption may occur through several overlapping mechanisms.

items.text: Competitive Attraction
Competitive Attraction
Dispensers act as numerous false pheromone sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dispensers act as numerous false pheromone sources.

items.text: Males move toward dispensers rather than females and expend time and energy tracking false sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Males move toward dispensers rather than females and expend time and energy tracking false sources.

items.text: Efficacy increases when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy increases when:

items.text: Signal Camouflage
Camouflage
Pheromone concentration in the air increases to a level at which the odour plume from a female becomes difficult to distinguish.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone concentration in the air increases to a level at which the odour plume from a female becomes difficult to distinguish.

items.text: Sensory Adaptation or Fatigue
Continuous exposure to high concentrations may temporarily reduce the responsiveness of olfactory receptors or male orientation behaviour.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Continuous exposure to high concentrations may temporarily reduce the responsiveness of olfactory receptors or male orientation behaviour.

items.text: Not all dispensers act through the same mechanism. Performance depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not all dispensers act through the same mechanism. Performance depends on:

items.text: Types of Mating-Disruption Dispensers
Passive Dispensers
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items.text: They release pheromone continuously through diffusion and evaporation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They release pheromone continuously through diffusion and evaporation.

items.text: Active Dispensers
These include devices that release pheromone:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include devices that release pheromone:

items.text: They use fewer release points but require:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They use fewer release points but require:

items: A power source or reliable device.

items.text: The number of passive dispensers cannot be compared directly with the number of aerosol devices because release rate per point and area of influence differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The number of passive dispensers cannot be compared directly with the number of aerosol devices because release rate per point and area of influence differ.

items.text: Timing of Mating-Disruption Installation
Dispensers should be installed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dispensers should be installed:

items: Before large numbers of mated females are present.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Before large numbers of mated females are present.

items.text: Delayed installation is a major cause of failure because disruption cannot reverse mating that has already occurred or kill larvae already inside the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Delayed installation is a major cause of failure because disruption cannot reverse mating that has already occurred or kill larvae already inside the plant.

items.text: When the program begins in a field with existing infestation, a corrective intervention targeting eggs and larvae may be required at the same time as dispenser installation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the program begins in a field with existing infestation, a corrective intervention targeting eggs and larvae may be required at the same time as dispenser installation.

items.text: Effect of Pest Density on Mating-Disruption Success
Mating-disruption efficacy is affected by pest density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating-disruption efficacy is affected by pest density.

items.text: At low density:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At low density:

items: The distance between males and females is relatively greater.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The distance between males and females is relatively greater.

items.text: At high density:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At high density:

items: Males and females are closer together.
Mating may occur without long-distance flight.
Males are more likely to reach females despite disruption.
A higher release rate or dispenser density may be required.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Males are more likely to reach females despite disruption.
[[prepared]]A higher release rate or dispenser density may be required.

items.text: Reducing the initial population is therefore a fundamental condition for successful mating disruption.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reducing the initial population is therefore a fundamental condition for successful mating disruption.

items.text: Spatial Containment
Early studies showed that mating disruption was more consistent in highly enclosed greenhouses than in poorly isolated structures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early studies showed that mating disruption was more consistent in highly enclosed greenhouses than in poorly isolated structures.

items.text: Protected structures reduce:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Protected structures reduce:

items: Rapid pheromone loss caused by wind.

items.text: In open fields, efficacy is more strongly affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In open fields, efficacy is more strongly affected by:

items.text: This does not mean mating disruption cannot be used in open fields. Some trials have recorded success, but results are more site- and system-dependent and should not be generalized without local testing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean mating disruption cannot be used in open fields. Some trials have recorded success, but results are more site- and system-dependent and should not be generalized without local testing.

items.text: Experimental Rates and Densities
Studies have used different systems that cannot be converted into one universal recommendation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have used different systems that cannot be converted into one universal recommendation.

items.text: Examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examples include:

items: 500 dispensers/hectare, each carrying approximately 60 mg, equivalent to an initial pheromone dose of about 30 g/hectare in highly enclosed greenhouses.
1,000 dispensers/hectare with a total dose of approximately 60 g/hectare in other studies.
675–900 tubular dispensers/hectare in a recent trial under high pest density.
Limited numbers of active aerosol devices instead of hundreds of passive dispensers.
Gel or point-source systems with different quantities and concentrations.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]500 dispensers/hectare, each carrying approximately 60 mg, equivalent to an initial pheromone dose of about 30 g/hectare in highly enclosed greenhouses.
[[prepared]]1,000 dispensers/hectare with a total dose of approximately 60 g/hectare in other studies.
[[prepared]]675–900 tubular dispensers/hectare in a recent trial under high pest density.
[[prepared]]Limited numbers of active aerosol devices instead of hundreds of passive dispensers.
[[prepared]]Gel or point-source systems with different quantities and concentrations.

items.text: Increasing dispenser number does not always increase efficacy proportionally because decisive factors include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing dispenser number does not always increase efficacy proportionally because decisive factors include:

items.text: The registered dose of an approved product in the country should be used. Experimental doses or products from other countries should not be transferred directly into commercial use.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The registered dose of an approved product in the country should be used. Experimental doses or products from other countries should not be transferred directly into commercial use.

items.text: Distribution of Dispensers
Dispensers should be distributed uniformly throughout the treated area while considering:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dispensers should be distributed uniformly throughout the treated area while considering:

items: Attachment to plants or supports according to product instructions.
Raising them as plants grow when necessary.
Avoidance of excessive clustering at one point.
Coverage of entrances and sides facing immigration sources.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Attachment to plants or supports according to product instructions.
[[prepared]]Raising them as plants grow when necessary.
[[prepared]]Avoidance of excessive clustering at one point.
[[prepared]]Coverage of entrances and sides facing immigration sources.

items.text: Irregular distribution creates low-concentration zones where mating may occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Irregular distribution creates low-concentration zones where mating may occur.

items.text: Effect of Wind and Ventilation
Air movement affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Air movement affects:

items: Formation of zones with high or low concentration.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Formation of zones with high or low concentration.

items.text: In open fields, wind may carry pheromone outside the treated area, particularly when the plot is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In open fields, wind may carry pheromone outside the treated area, particularly when the plot is:

items.text: In greenhouses, poor ventilation may cause pheromone accumulation, whereas large openings may accelerate pheromone loss and permit entry of mated females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In greenhouses, poor ventilation may cause pheromone accumulation, whereas large openings may accelerate pheromone loss and permit entry of mated females.

items.text: Treated Area
Mating disruption generally becomes more effective as treated area increases and the ratio of edge to interior decreases.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption generally becomes more effective as treated area increases and the ratio of edge to interior decreases.

items.text: Small plots face problems including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Small plots face problems including:

items: Difficulty in forming a uniform pheromone environment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Difficulty in forming a uniform pheromone environment.

items.text: Application across:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Application across:

items.text: Is more logical than application in a small field surrounded by untreated infestation sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Is more logical than application in a small field surrounded by untreated infestation sources.

items.text: Edge Reinforcement
Dispenser density may be increased relatively along exposed edges, particularly those:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dispenser density may be increased relatively along exposed edges, particularly those:

items.text: However, edge reinforcement does not compensate for poor internal distribution or continued immigration of large numbers of mated females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, edge reinforcement does not compensate for poor internal distribution or continued immigration of large numbers of mated females.

items.text: Evaluation of Mating-Disruption Success
A reduction in male catch in pheromone traps is used as an indicator of disrupted communication and is sometimes called:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A reduction in male catch in pheromone traps is used as an indicator of disrupted communication and is sometimes called:

items.text: Trap Shutdown

However, trap shutdown or reduced catch alone does not prove successful control. Widespread pheromone may prevent males from reaching monitoring traps while some short-range mating continues within the canopy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, trap shutdown or reduced catch alone does not prove successful control. Widespread pheromone may prevent males from reaching monitoring traps while some short-range mating continues within the canopy.

items.text: Evaluation should therefore include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation should therefore include:

items: Distribution of infestation between edges and interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Distribution of infestation between edges and interior.

items.text: Reduction in plant infestation and the following generation provides the true evidence of program success.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduction in plant infestation and the following generation provides the true evidence of program success.

items.text: Traps Within Mating-Disruption Areas
Dedicated pheromone traps are used to assess mating disruption, but their readings differ from normal monitoring.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dedicated pheromone traps are used to assess mating disruption, but their readings differ from normal monitoring.

items.text: Low catch may indicate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Low catch may indicate:

items.text: The following should therefore be reviewed together:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should therefore be reviewed together:

items.text: Internal traps alone should not be used to estimate the true male population during mating disruption.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Internal traps alone should not be used to estimate the true male population during mating disruption.

items.text: Duration of Dispenser Effectiveness
Effective life varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Effective life varies according to:

items.text: Some dispensers may persist through a short crop cycle, whereas other systems require replacement or reapplication.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some dispensers may persist through a short crop cycle, whereas other systems require replacement or reapplication.

items.text: The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items.text: An increase in infestation should not be the first indication that dispenser life has ended.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An increase in infestation should not be the first indication that dispenser life has ended.

items.text: Mating Disruption in Open Fields
Trials in open fields have produced variable results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trials in open fields have produced variable results.

items.text: Early trials recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early trials recorded:

items: A major reduction in male access to traps.
Without a comparable reduction in leaf or fruit infestation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A major reduction in male access to traps.
[[prepared]]Without a comparable reduction in leaf or fruit infestation.

items.text: This was often associated with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This was often associated with:

items.text: Later trials in particular areas and under specific systems recorded reduced infestation when appropriate mating-disruption systems were used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Later trials in particular areas and under specific systems recorded reduced infestation when appropriate mating-disruption systems were used.

items.text: Mating disruption in open fields should therefore be classified as a promising, site- and system-dependent technique rather than a fixed general recommendation for every field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption in open fields should therefore be classified as a promising, site- and system-dependent technique rather than a fixed general recommendation for every field.

items.text: Mating Disruption in Greenhouses
The probability of success is higher in greenhouses when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The probability of success is higher in greenhouses when:

items.text: Studies have recorded substantial reductions in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have recorded substantial reductions in:

items.text: However, mating disruption cannot compensate for infested transplants or residues from the previous crop remaining inside the greenhouse.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, mating disruption cannot compensate for infested transplants or residues from the previous crop remaining inside the greenhouse.

items.text: Combining Mating Disruption with Biological Control
Pheromones do not have direct insecticidal toxicity comparable to broad-spectrum insecticides and can therefore be integrated with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromones do not have direct insecticidal toxicity comparable to broad-spectrum insecticides and can therefore be integrated with:

items.text: Mating disruption targets adult communication and mating, whereas natural enemies target eggs and larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption targets adult communication and mating, whereas natural enemies target eggs and larvae.

items.text: The combined approach therefore covers several stages of the pest life cycle.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combined approach therefore covers several stages of the pest life cycle.

items.text: Nevertheless, pheromones should not be assumed to be behaviourally neutral to all natural enemies. Experimental studies have reported responses by some Trichogramma populations to host pheromone or behavioural changes after exposure. Compatibility should therefore be evaluated within the local program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nevertheless, pheromones should not be assumed to be behaviourally neutral to all natural enemies. Experimental studies have reported responses by some Trichogramma populations to host pheromone or behavioural changes after exposure. Compatibility should therefore be evaluated within the local program.

items.text: Combining Mating Disruption with Insecticides
Mating disruption may require complementary intervention when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption may require complementary intervention when:

items: The program begins after infestation is already present.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The program begins after infestation is already present.

items.text: Complementary intervention should preferably target:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Complementary intervention should preferably target:

items: Stages not directly affected by mating disruption.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Stages not directly affected by mating disruption.

items.text: Mating disruption should not be used to justify calendar spraying. Its purpose is to reduce reliance on insecticides while maintaining acceptable infestation levels.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption should not be used to justify calendar spraying. Its purpose is to reduce reliance on insecticides while maintaining acceptable infestation levels.

items.text: Attract-and-Kill
Attract-and-Kill
Attract-and-kill combines an attractant with a toxicant at a defined point. Males are attracted to the point and exposed to the active ingredient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Attract-and-kill combines an attractant with a toxicant at a defined point. Males are attracted to the point and exposed to the [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]].

items.text: Unlike a trap, the insect may not be physically retained in a container but may contact:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Unlike a trap, the insect may not be physically retained in a container but may contact:

items: A droplet or point containing insecticide.

items.text: Potential benefits include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Potential benefits include:

items: Use of a small quantity of insecticide.
Lower exposure of non-target organisms when correctly designed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Use of a small quantity of insecticide.
[[prepared]]Lower exposure of non-target organisms when correctly designed.

items.text: However, efficacy is affected by many of the same limitations as mass trapping:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, efficacy is affected by many of the same limitations as mass trapping:

items: Degradation of the attractant or toxicant.

items.text: Conditions Required for Attract-and-Kill
An attract-and-kill point should provide:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An attract-and-kill point should provide:

items: Attraction that competes successfully with natural females.
Low risk of insecticide transfer to the crop.
Legal registration for the intended use.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Attraction that competes successfully with natural females.
[[prepared]]Low risk of insecticide transfer to the crop.

items.text: Pheromone should not be mixed with an insecticide improvisationally because formulation, dose, and carrier determine both efficacy and safety.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone should not be mixed with an insecticide improvisationally because formulation, dose, and carrier determine both efficacy and safety.

items.text: Position of Attract-and-Kill Within the Program
Attract-and-kill remains less widely used than traps and mating disruption, and its performance depends on the specific product and system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Attract-and-kill remains less widely used than traps and mating disruption, and its performance depends on the specific product and system.

items.text: It should therefore be treated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should therefore be treated as:

items: Requiring a designed and registered product.
Not suitable for on-farm manufacture by mixing insecticide with a pheromone lure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Not suitable for on-farm manufacture by mixing insecticide with a pheromone lure.

items.text: Results from one gel product should not be generalized to all attract-and-kill systems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results from one gel product should not be generalized to all attract-and-kill systems.

items.text: Plant Volatiles and Female Attractants
Female attractants are important because capturing females may directly reduce oviposition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Female attractants are important because capturing females may directly reduce oviposition.

items.text: Volatile compounds from tomato and host plants are being studied, including odours released by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Volatile compounds from tomato and host plants are being studied, including odours released by:

items.text: Females may respond to some compounds while searching for oviposition sites.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Females may respond to some compounds while searching for oviposition sites.

items.text: However, development of a reliable commercial female lure requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, development of a reliable commercial female lure requires:

items: Differentiation between attraction to a plant and attraction into a trap.
Avoidance of attracting females into the crop without retaining them.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Differentiation between attraction to a plant and attraction into a trap.
[[prepared]]Avoidance of attracting females into the crop without retaining them.

items.text: Female attractants remain less ready for commercial application than male-targeted sex pheromones.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Female attractants remain less ready for commercial application than male-targeted sex pheromones.

items.text: Light Traps
Light traps use attraction to a light source of a particular wavelength.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Light traps use attraction to a light source of a particular wavelength.

items.text: They may capture both male and female Tuta absoluta, but their use faces scientific and environmental limitations including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may capture both male and female Tuta absoluta, but their use faces scientific and environmental limitations including:

items: Effects of surrounding illumination and moonlight.

items.text: Light traps are therefore not standard replacements for pheromone traps and should not be incorporated into a large commercial program without evaluating their effects on non-target organisms.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Light traps are therefore not standard replacements for pheromone traps and should not be incorporated into a large commercial program without evaluating their effects on non-target organisms.

items.text: Compatibility of Light and Pheromone Traps
Combining light and pheromone may increase moth catches in some experiments, but increased catch does not necessarily prove improved control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Combining light and pheromone may increase moth catches in some experiments, but increased catch does not necessarily prove improved control.

items.text: Evaluation should include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation should include:

items.text: The combination should not be recommended merely because a larger number of insects is captured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combination should not be recommended merely because a larger number of insects is captured.

items.text: Effect of Weather on Pheromone-Based Methods
Temperature affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Temperature affects:

items.text: Wind affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Wind affects:

items.text: Other effects include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Other effects include:

items.text: Lures and dispensers may therefore require more rapid replacement under hot conditions, according to product data and monitoring results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Lures and dispensers may therefore require more rapid replacement under hot conditions, according to product data and monitoring results.

items.text: Interference Among Traps and Dispensers
Interference may occur when traps or dispensers are placed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Interference may occur when traps or dispensers are placed:

items.text: Interference may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Interference may cause:

items: Male attraction to an area between traps.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Male attraction to an area between traps.

items.text: Monitoring traps, mass-trapping devices, and mating-disruption dispensers should be clearly separated, with the location and function of each tool recorded.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring traps, mass-trapping devices, and mating-disruption dispensers should be clearly separated, with the location and function of each tool recorded.

items.text: Quality of Pheromone Lures
Lures should be evaluated according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Lures should be evaluated according to:

items.text: Lures should not be used when they are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Lures should not be used when they are:

items: Not intended for the target species.

items.text: Weak lure performance may reduce catches and be misinterpreted as a genuine population decline.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weak lure performance may reduce catches and be misinterpreted as a genuine population decline.

items.text: Storage of Lures
Lures should be stored according to manufacturer instructions, usually:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Lures should be stored according to manufacturer instructions, usually:

items: Separate from insecticides and strong odours.

items.text: The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items.text: Lures should not be left in a vehicle or hot storage area before use.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Lures should not be left in a vehicle or hot storage area before use.

items.text: Indicators of Successful Mass Trapping
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: A gradual decline in male numbers.
Absence of increasing infestation along field edges.
Continued trap operation without drying or saturation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Absence of increasing infestation along field edges.
[[prepared]]Continued trap operation without drying or saturation.

items.text: Recording a large number of captured males alone is not sufficient as an independent indicator of success.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recording a large number of captured males alone is not sufficient as an independent indicator of success.

items.text: Indicators of Successful Mating Disruption
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: Reduced male access to test traps.
Reduction in mines in the following generation.
Uniform reduction between field edges and interior.
Continued dispenser efficacy throughout the protection period.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduction in mines in the following generation.
[[prepared]]Uniform reduction between field edges and interior.
[[prepared]]Continued dispenser efficacy throughout the protection period.

items.text: Indicators of Mating-Disruption Failure
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: Continued high male catch within the treated area.
Continued increase in eggs and larvae.
Concentration of infestation along field edges.
High pest density before program initiation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Continued high male catch within the treated area.

items.text: Failure should not automatically be attributed to poor pheromone quality because it may result from program design, timing, immigration, or pest density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Failure should not automatically be attributed to poor pheromone quality because it may result from program design, timing, immigration, or pest density.

items.text: Mass-Trapping Record Template
The record should be linked with data on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The record should be linked with data on:

items.text: Mating-Disruption Record Template
Strategy Selection

rows: Low pest density and relatively isolated field
Well-sealed greenhouse and low pest density
Small open field near infested fields
Do not rely on mating disruption alone
Reduce population first, then apply behavioural method
Immigration wave from an old field
Manage the source and field edges
Use pheromones compatible with natural-enemy monitoring
Evaluate economic feasibility and speed of effect
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Low pest density and relatively isolated field
[[prepared]]Do not rely on mating disruption alone
[[prepared]]Reduce population first, then apply behavioural method
[[prepared]]Evaluate economic feasibility and speed of effect

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Using One Monitoring Trap and Describing It as Mass Trapping
Mass trapping requires a dense network and high-capacity traps.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping requires a dense network and high-capacity traps.

items.text: Error Two: Treating the Number of Captured Moths as Direct Evidence of Reduced Damage
Eggs, larvae, and fruit infestation must be measured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs, larvae, and fruit infestation must be measured.

items.text: Error Three: Starting Mass Trapping After Infestation Has Increased
A large proportion of females may already have mated and laid eggs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large proportion of females may already have mated and laid eggs.

items.text: Error Four: Using Mass Trapping Alone
Immature stages and immigration sources remain untargeted.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Immature stages and immigration sources remain untargeted.

items.text: Error Five: Using a Fixed Trap Number Under All Conditions
Requirements vary with area, isolation, and trap type.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Requirements vary with area, isolation, and trap type.

items.text: Error Six: Neglecting Dry Water Traps
A dry trap does not retain males.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A dry trap does not retain males.

items.text: Error Seven: Installing Mating Disruption After the Outbreak Begins
It does not affect eggs and larvae already present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It does not affect eggs and larvae already present.

items.text: Error Eight: Applying Mating Disruption in a Small Area Surrounded by Infested Fields
Immigration of mated females and edge effects increase.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Immigration of mated females and edge effects increase.

items.text: Error Nine: Treating Trap Shutdown as Sufficient Evidence of Success
Mating may continue despite low trap catch.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating may continue despite low trap catch.

items.text: Error Ten: Copying a Dose from a Foreign Study
Products, release rates, and registration conditions differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Products, release rates, and registration conditions differ.

items.text: Error Eleven: Irregular Dispenser Distribution
This creates zones with low pheromone concentration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This creates zones with low pheromone concentration.

items.text: Error Twelve: Neglecting Field Edges
Edges are the areas most exposed to immigration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Edges are the areas most exposed to immigration.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Mixing Pheromone with Insecticide to Produce a Homemade Attract-and-Kill Product
Only a designed and registered formulation should be used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Only a designed and registered formulation should be used.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Relying on Non-Specific Light Traps
They may affect beneficial insects and do not provide selective control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may affect beneficial insects and do not provide selective control.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Poor Lure Storage
This may cause loss of active material before use.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may cause loss of active material before use.

items.text: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
The priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The priorities are:

items: 1. Use pheromone traps primarily for monitoring and warning.
2. Do not consider one trap per feddan a mass-trapping program.
3. Begin any mass-trapping program before pest density increases.
4. Increase trap density along high-risk edges.
6. Link male catches with inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits.
7. Do not rely on mass trapping alone.
8. Do not generalize mating disruption to open fields without local testing.
9. Apply mating disruption over the largest contiguous area possible.
10. Reduce initial pest density before or at the start of disruption.
11. Manage old fields and sources of mated females.
12. Use the registered product dose rather than directly copying experimental doses.
13. Evaluate field edges and interior separately.
14. Continue plant inspection even when male trap catch stops.
15. Integrate pheromones with natural enemies and selective products.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Use pheromone traps primarily for monitoring and warning.
[[prepared]]2. Do not consider one trap per feddan a mass-trapping program.
[[prepared]]3. Begin any mass-trapping program before pest density increases.
[[prepared]]4. Increase trap density along high-risk edges.
[[prepared]]6. Link male catches with inspection of eggs, mines, and fruits.
[[prepared]]7. Do not rely on mass trapping alone.
[[prepared]]8. Do not generalize mating disruption to open fields without local testing.
[[prepared]]9. Apply mating disruption over the largest contiguous area possible.
[[prepared]]10. Reduce initial pest density before or at the start of disruption.
[[prepared]]11. Manage old fields and sources of mated females.
[[prepared]]12. Use the registered product dose rather than directly copying experimental doses.
[[prepared]]13. Evaluate field edges and interior separately.
[[prepared]]14. Continue plant inspection even when male trap catch stops.
[[prepared]]15. Integrate pheromones with natural enemies and selective products.

items.text: Summary of Section Seven
Behavioural control of Tuta absoluta depends mainly on the female sex pheromone, which consists of a principal component:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Behavioural control of Tuta absoluta depends mainly on the female sex pheromone, which consists of a principal component:

items.text: And a secondary component:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And a secondary component:

items.text: E3Z8-14:Ac
These compounds are used in monitoring, mass trapping, mating disruption, and attract-and-kill.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These compounds are used in monitoring, mass trapping, mating disruption, and attract-and-kill.

items.text: Low-density monitoring traps should be distinguished from mass trapping, which requires a larger network of traps.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Low-density monitoring traps should be distinguished from mass trapping, which requires a larger network of traps.

items.text: Historical advisory mass-trapping densities of approximately 40–50 traps per hectare have been reported for open fields, equivalent to about 17–21 traps per feddan. However, this density is not a fixed rule and should be evaluated according to trap type, infestation level, and immigration pressure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Historical advisory mass-trapping densities of approximately 40–50 traps per hectare have been reported for open fields, equivalent to about 17–21 traps per feddan. However, this density is not a fixed rule and should be evaluated according to trap type, infestation level, and immigration pressure.

items.text: Mass trapping does not always provide sufficient damage reduction when used alone because the pheromone attracts males only, males can mate several times, and mated females can immigrate from outside.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mass trapping does not always provide sufficient damage reduction when used alone because the pheromone attracts males only, males can mate several times, and mated females can immigrate from outside.

items.text: Mating disruption depends on preventing males from locating females and performs best when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mating disruption depends on preventing males from locating females and performs best when:

items: The treated area is large or the structure is well sealed.
Immigration of mated females is prevented.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The treated area is large or the structure is well sealed.

items.text: Several greenhouse studies under high containment have achieved successful results using different dispenser rates and densities, whereas results have been less consistent in open greenhouses and open fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Several greenhouse studies under high containment have achieved successful results using different dispenser rates and densities, whereas results have been less consistent in open greenhouses and open fields.

items.text: Trap shutdown alone is not sufficient evidence of successful mating disruption. A reduction in eggs, larvae, mines, and fruit infestation must also be demonstrated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap shutdown alone is not sufficient evidence of successful mating disruption. A reduction in eggs, larvae, mines, and fruit infestation must also be demonstrated.

items.text: Plant-based female attractants and attract-and-kill techniques remain promising, but are less mature and less widely used than traps and mating disruption and require registered products and local evaluation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant-based female attractants and attract-and-kill techniques remain promising, but are less mature and less widely used than traps and mating disruption and require registered products and local evaluation.
